    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-readme.md",
      "title": "XanaNode Protocol README",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "The main human-readable overview of the XanaNode protocol.",
      "artifact_path": "README.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/README.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# XanaNode\r\n\r\n![XanaNode Logo](media/images/xananode-icon.svg)\r\n\r\n## What is XanaNode?\n\nShort:\n\nRelationships preserve knowledge.\n\nCanonical:\n\nXanaNode is a protocol for independently authored knowledge substrates that preserve relationships, provenance, lineage, disagreement, and addressable fragments, so knowledge can move across tools and media without losing its structure.\n\nExpanded:\n\nXanaNode is a protocol for independently authoring, transmitting, and federating knowledge substrates that preserve relationships, provenance, lineage, disagreement, and addressable fragments, enabling durable human-to-machine knowledge transfer through many possible projection layers.\n\r\nRather than treating documents as the primary unit of knowledge, XanaNode treats relationships as first-class entities and models knowledge as a connected network of people, concepts, claims, questions, hypotheses, problems, knowledge gaps, communications, responses, sources, events, media, organizations, technologies, and their relationships.\n\r\nXanaNode is designed to be both human-readable and machine-interpretable.\n\nXanaNode is also a working software stack. The protocol, Core SDK, Workspace engine, Studio workbench, Mobile capture companion, Hugo projection layer, and canonical XanaNode.com substrate are designed to run together and to describe themselves as XanaNode data. The canonical substrate is not only documentation about the project; it is a living example of the protocol carrying its own schemas, registries, media assets, source snapshots, project history, implementation links, build metadata, governance notes, and unresolved work.\n\nThis repository now also publishes an explicit substrate source at `substrate-source/`. That folder is generated from the protocol repository itself so the rest of the stack can federate with the protocol as a normal substrate instead of treating this repository as a private implementation detail.\n\nThat recursive quality is intentional. If XanaNode says relationships preserve knowledge, the XanaNode project itself must preserve the relationships that explain XanaNode: why files exist, where schemas came from, which tools consume them, what changed, what is pending, and how the public website was produced.\n\nDocumentation is licensed under `CC-BY-4.0`. Schemas, validators, and reference code are licensed under `Apache-2.0`. The XanaNode name and logo are trademarks of the XanaNode project; see [LICENSE.md](LICENSE.md) and [TRADEMARK.md](TRADEMARK.md).\n\nImplementations should identify themselves as XanaNode-compatible and link to the canonical specification when accurate:\n\n```text\nThis project is a XanaNode-compatible implementation.\nCanonical specification: https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode\n```\n\nIt is not a wiki.\n\r\nIt is not a graph database.\r\n\r\nIt is not a note-taking application.\r\n\r\nIt is a protocol for knowledge substrates and the rules that let them move, federate, and be projected without losing their structure.\n\nFragments are not limited to paragraph chunks. The protocol's fragment/tumbler model can also describe quoted spans, character or word ranges, page regions, and time-based media segments. The stable address is the tumbler; the selector carries the granular \"where inside this source version\" detail.\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## The Core Problem\r\n\r\nHumanity has become exceptionally good at publishing information.\r\n\r\nWe have become far less successful at preserving the relationships that give information meaning.\r\n\r\nModern publishing systems prioritize documents:\r\n\r\n* web pages\r\n* articles\r\n* books\r\n* PDFs\r\n* databases\r\n\r\nYet knowledge does not exist inside isolated documents.\r\n\r\nKnowledge exists in:\r\n\r\n* provenance\r\n* context\r\n* lineage\r\n* contradiction\r\n* influence\r\n* evidence\r\n* explanation\r\n* association\r\n\r\nThe result is a civilization-scale memory problem.\r\n\r\nInformation survives.\r\n\r\nRelationships disappear.\r\n\r\nSources become detached from conclusions.\r\n\r\nClaims lose context.\r\n\r\nLinks decay.\r\n\r\nAuthority becomes difficult to evaluate.\r\n\r\nThe problem becomes increasingly visible in artificial intelligence systems, but it predates AI by decades.\r\n\r\nXanaNode is an attempt to address this deeper problem.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# Core Principles\r\n\r\n## 1. Relationships Are First-Class\r\n\r\nTraditional systems primarily store content.\r\n\r\nXanaNode stores both content and the relationships that connect content.\r\n\r\nExamples:\r\n\r\n* supports\r\n* contradicts\r\n* explains\r\n* derived_from\r\n* created\n* memorializes\r\n* documents\r\n* participates_in\r\n\r\nRelationships are explicit, queryable, versioned structures.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## 2. Provenance Matters\r\n\r\nEvery claim should be traceable.\r\n\r\nUsers should be able to answer:\r\n\r\n* Where did this come from?\r\n* Who said it?\r\n* What evidence supports it?\r\n* What contradicts it?\r\n* What changed over time?\r\n\r\nXanaNode prioritizes preserving knowledge lineage rather than merely preserving information.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## 3. Claims Are Not Facts\r\n\r\nClaims, observations, concepts, essays, and sources are distinct node types.\r\n\r\nXanaNode does not assume consensus.\r\n\r\nInstead it preserves:\n\n* claims\n* counterclaims\n* evidence\n* disagreement\n* uncertainty\n* open questions\n* hypotheses\n* knowledge gaps\n* review status\n\nThe goal is not enforced truth.\n\nThe goal is navigable knowledge.\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## 4. Knowledge Is Structure\r\n\r\nA knowledge substrate is not information.\r\n\r\nA knowledge substrate is the structure that determines how information relates, persists, evolves, and can be understood.\r\n\r\nThe same information arranged differently produces different knowledge capabilities.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n## 5. Human Readable and Machine Interpretable\r\n\r\nMost semantic systems optimize for machines.\r\n\r\nMost publishing systems optimize for humans.\r\n\r\nXanaNode attempts to support both.\r\n\r\nHumans should be able to browse, understand, and author the graph.\r\n\r\nMachines should be able to query, reason over, and analyze it.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# Knowledge Substrates\n\nA knowledge substrate is an independently authored graph of knowledge.\n\nIn XanaNode, a production substrate is backed by a Git repository. Git supplies history, branching, review, merge, and synchronization; XanaNode supplies the knowledge model, schemas, validation rules, and federation semantics.\n\nA substrate is portable. A hand-built substrate should be able to follow the standard folder tree, include its own files, and open in compatible tools without private state. A `.substrate` archive carries the whole substrate as data: manifest, nodes, relationships, media, source files, schemas, reports, and workspace metadata needed for round trips.\n\nExamples:\n\r\n* personal knowledge substrates\r\n* research substrates\r\n* organizational substrates\r\n* historical archives\r\n* educational collections\r\n* institutional memory systems\r\n\r\nEach substrate remains independently owned and moderated.\n\nThere is no requirement for a central authority.\n\n## Repository Files, Assets, And Knowledge Objects\n\nXanaNode does not treat every file in a Git repository as equally worthy of becoming its own node.\n\nPortable substrates should preserve important local files, but preservation and elevation are different actions:\n\n* **Preserve as an attached asset** when the file is implementation support, packaging glue, projection inventory, test scaffolding, or another file whose meaning is already carried by a higher-level node.\n* **Promote to a first-class node** when the file is itself a knowledge-bearing object that should be citable, discussable, governable, reviewable, or traversable on its own.\n\nA raw repository file generally deserves its own node when one or more of these are true:\n\n* it defines protocol, schema, registry, compatibility, or governance behavior\n* it is an independently citable specification, proposal, policy, source document, or canonical example\n* it is itself a portable substrate/report/registry artifact that other tools may inspect directly\n* authors are likely to discuss its contents semantically, not merely acknowledge that the file exists\n\nA raw repository file should usually remain an attached asset, snapshot, or supporting file when:\n\n* it exists mainly to make another node render, build, test, package, or execute\n* it is one item in a large projection-asset inventory such as icons, thumbnails, static images, or generated output\n* it is better modeled as provenance on a higher-level source, media, project, schema, or substrate node\n\nThis rule also applies to source/media modeling. A file does not need two separate nodes just because it is both readable and visual.\n\n* If the knowledge object being cited is the file itself, prefer one `source` node.\n* If that same node carries a local image, PDF, audio, video, or other file, it may also carry the `media` facet and related `media_type` fields.\n* Use a separate `media` node only when the media object is distinct from the source object it previews, documents, depicts, or was derived from.\n\nThe practical goal is simple: preserve the bytes, but only promote the files that carry real semantic weight.\n\n---\n\r\n# Federation\r\n\r\nXanaNode is designed for federation.\r\n\r\nMultiple independent substrates can interoperate without surrendering ownership.\r\n\r\nExample:\r\n\r\nResearcher A maintains a substrate.\r\n\r\nResearcher B maintains a substrate.\r\n\r\nA university maintains a substrate.\r\n\r\nA museum maintains a substrate.\r\n\r\nEach can preserve its own interpretations and governance policies.\r\n\r\nFederation allows these substrates to be connected and analyzed together.\r\n\r\nThe goal is not one global database.\r\n\r\nThe goal is a network of interoperable knowledge substrates.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# Schema Architecture\n\r\nXanaNode uses a layered schema model.\r\n\r\n## Core Schema\r\n\r\nProvides the canonical node and relationship registries for the protocol.\r\n\r\nThe current core registry files are versioned and live in [schemas/](schemas):\r\n\r\n* [xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json](schemas/xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json)\r\n* [xananode-node-types.schema.v0.3.0.json](schemas/xananode-node-types.schema.v0.3.0.json)\r\n* [xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json](schemas/xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json)\n* [xananode-relationship-types.schema.v0.5.0.json](schemas/xananode-relationship-types.schema.v0.5.0.json)\n\r\nThe node registry currently centers on core types such as person, concept, claim, question, hypothesis, problem, knowledge_gap, communication, response, source, essay, and observation.\n\nThe relationship registry currently centers on core types such as defines, has_claim, supports, contradicts, documents, derived_from, answers, investigates, requires_source, possibly_related_to, and communicated_to.\n\nThe core property registry standardizes common open properties such as `uncertainty_level`, `review_status`, `research_priority`, `evidence_strength`, `communication_model`, and `communication_pattern`.\n\nThe substrate layout registry defines the standard folder tree that compatible tools should understand:\n\n* [substrate-layout.schema.json](schemas/substrate-layout.schema.json)\n* [xananode-substrate-layout.v0.1.0.json](schemas/xananode-substrate-layout.v0.1.0.json)\n\nThis makes the substrate format tight enough that someone can manually build a substrate outside the XanaNode tools, drop it into Core, Workspace, Studio, Hugo, or another compatible implementation, and expect the records and files to be discoverable.\n\r\nThe core schema provides interoperability while still allowing extension schemas to define namespaced custom types.\r\n\r\nValidation tools live in [tools/](tools). The repository validator checks JSON Schema conformance plus XanaNode-specific integrity rules such as declared relationship types, registered namespaces, and resolvable relationship endpoints.\n\n---\n\n# Implementations\n\nThe protocol repository is implementation-neutral. It defines the canonical schemas, registries, examples, governance material, and validation rules. The active implementation stack is maintained in separate repositories that consume this protocol:\n\n* [XanaNode Core SDK](https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Core-SDK) is the renderer-independent parser, validator, graph builder, fragment engine, and exporter. It includes this protocol repository as `vendor/xananode-protocol`.\n* [XanaNode Workspace](https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Workspace) is the local-first workspace engine used by Studio, CLIs, and editor integrations. It includes Core SDK as `vendor/xananode-core`.\n* [XanaNode Hugo Theme](https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Hugo) is the static-site renderer and graph viewer for XanaNode-compatible substrates.\n* [XanaNode Studio](https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Studio) is the desktop authoring workbench. It includes Hugo and Workspace as submodules.\n* [XanaNode Mobile](https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Mobile) is the Android capture companion for field intake, media capture, quick notes, portable `.substrate` handoff, and later federation back into the rest of the stack.\n\nThe intended dependency chain is:\n\n```text\nxananode protocol\n  -> XanaNode-Core-SDK\n    -> XanaNode-Workspace\n      -> XanaNode-Studio\n      -> XanaNode-Mobile\n\nXanaNode-Hugo consumes the protocol artifacts as the published/static preview renderer.\n```\n\nFor a full local Studio checkout:\n\n```bash\ngit clone --recurse-submodules https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Studio.git\ncd XanaNode-Studio\nnpm install\nnpm test\n```\n\n---\n\n## How The Stack Fits Together\n\nXanaNode is designed so the same substrate can move through the whole stack without being reinvented at each layer.\n\n```text\nProtocol defines the rules\nCore reads, validates, analyzes, and exports substrates\nWorkspace manages substrate folders, registry targets, working copies, and .substrate bundles\nStudio uses Workspace/Core for authoring and federation UX\nMobile uses Workspace/Core for capture-first intake and portable handoff\nHugo uses Core to validate and mount substrate sources, then projects them as a static site\n```\n\nOnce you are past Core, the thing is still a substrate. Sometimes it is a Git-backed substrate folder. Sometimes it is a portable `.substrate` bundle. Sometimes it is a mounted registry target cloned locally. These are transport and governance forms of the same thing, not separate knowledge objects.\n\nThe protocol registry can list known federation targets. Official tools should prefer that registry when offering online substrate choices, while still allowing federation with valid external substrates that are not yet listed there.\n\nTo regenerate the Protocol repo's own substrate source:\n\n```powershell\nnode tools/build-substrate-source.mjs\n```\n\nOr from the `XanaNode-Master` workspace root:\n\n```powershell\nnpm run protocol:build-substrate-source\n```\n\nThe protocol itself is not tied to Node.js. The current reference stack happens to be Node-based, but the protocol-facing contract should be callable from any stack. Official tooling should converge on stable `xananode-core` and `xananode-workspace` executable interfaces so Studio, Mobile, Hugo helpers, AI agents, and future third-party tools can operate on substrates without embedding one implementation language directly. See [specs/substrates.md](specs/substrates.md) and [proposals/XNP-0012-executable-tool-contract.md](proposals/XNP-0012-executable-tool-contract.md).\n\n## FAQ\n\n### Is this a real working stack?\n\nYes. XanaNode is not just a schema set. The protocol, Core SDK, Workspace engine, Studio authoring tool, Mobile capture companion, Hugo projection layer, and canonical XanaNode substrate are meant to run together right now.\n\n### What is a `.substrate` file?\n\nA `.substrate` file is a portable bundled substrate. It can carry the manifest, nodes, relationships, media, source snapshots, schemas, and supporting metadata needed to move a substrate between tools without losing pieces.\n\n### Is a `.substrate` different from a substrate folder?\n\nNo. It is the same substrate in a packaged transmission form. A Git-backed folder is better for active authorship and history. A `.substrate` bundle is better for shipping, handoff, offline import, and archival release.\n\n### Where does federation happen?\n\nFederation begins at the protocol level and is enforced through Core. Workspace is the management layer that should clone, mount, compare, import, or merge external substrates. Studio is the human-facing authoring interface for that workflow. Hugo is a projection layer that can mount configured substrate sources at build time and render the result.\n\n### Do I have to rewrite imported substrate data as Markdown for Hugo?\n\nNo. Markdown is an authoring convenience, not the only source form. Hugo should be able to mount or import validated substrate JSON through Core and render it directly.\n\n### Can substrates overlap?\n\nYes. Overlap is expected. Independent substrates may describe the same people, organizations, concepts, or works differently. XanaNode is meant to preserve that overlap, carry the provenance, and let tools surface the comparison instead of flattening it away.\n\n---\n\n## Extension Schema\n\nSubstrates may define custom node types and relationship types.\n\r\nExamples:\r\n\r\nmuseum:artifact\r\n\r\nbiology:species\r\n\r\nlegal:precedent\r\n\r\ngenealogy:ancestor_of\r\n\r\nCustom types are:\r\n\r\n* namespaced\r\n* documented\r\n* versioned\r\n* machine-readable\r\n\r\nThis allows evolution without breaking compatibility.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# Namespaces\r\n\r\nXanaNode supports schema namespaces.\r\n\r\nExamples:\r\n\r\nxananode:claim\r\n\r\nmuseum:artifact\r\n\r\nresearch:experiment\r\n\r\nNamespaces prevent collisions and allow independent schema development.\r\n\r\nRelationships between schemas can themselves be represented.\r\n\r\nExamples:\r\n\r\nequivalent_to\r\n\r\nbroader_than\r\n\r\nnarrower_than\r\n\r\nrelated_to\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# Tumbler Addressing\r\n\r\nXanaNode is intended to support persistent, location-independent addressing.\r\n\r\nThe goal is to move beyond URLs and document paths.\r\n\r\nObjects should remain addressable even if:\r\n\r\n* files move\r\n* websites migrate\r\n* structures change\r\n\r\nFuture implementations may support:\r\n\r\n* node-level addressing\r\n* relationship-level addressing\r\n* claim-level addressing\r\n* fragment-level addressing\r\n* transcluded content references\r\n\r\nThis provides durable references across time.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# Transclusion\r\n\r\nXanaNode embraces transclusion principles inspired by Project Xanadu.\r\n\r\nRather than duplicating information repeatedly, knowledge should be referenced and reused.\r\n\r\nBenefits include:\r\n\r\n* provenance preservation\r\n* reduction of duplication\r\n* easier revision tracking\r\n* stronger lineage visibility\r\n\r\nPractical implementations may initially focus on partial transclusion while preserving compatibility with existing publishing systems.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# Mergeability\r\n\r\nMergeability is a core design goal.\r\n\r\nTwo substrates should be able to exchange knowledge without requiring identical governance.\r\n\r\nMerge operations should preserve:\r\n\r\n* authorship\r\n* provenance\r\n* disagreement\r\n* source attribution\r\n* local moderation decisions\r\n\r\nMerging should add structure rather than destroy context.\r\n\r\n---\r\n\r\n# Civilizational Memory\r\n\r\nThe long-term goal of XanaNode is not merely better note-taking, search, or AI retrieval.\r\n\r\nThe goal is the creation of a federated civilizational memory system.\r\n\r\nA system where:\r\n\r\n* knowledge remains locally owned\r\n* provenance remains visible\r\n* disagreement remains preserved\r\n* schemas remain interoperable\r\n* relationships remain navigable\r\n\r\nA civilization-scale memory substrate built from independently maintained knowledge systems rather than a single centralized authority.\r\n\r\nXanaNode is an open protocol for building interoperable knowledge substrates that preserve relationships, provenance, lineage, and disagreement across independently authored and federated knowledge systems.\r\n\r\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/README.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:f098ae56c4c3b8a81bb8c32c54c91b31d2daa5de9ff9eae1bb305a6663c7427d",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-27T16:35:32.999Z",

    {

      "content": "# Registry\n\nThe registry directory lists known namespaces, schemas, implementations, and related resources.\n\nThis is not a central authority over truth.\n\nIt is a discovery aid.\n\nKnown implementations are listed in [known-implementations.json](known-implementations.json). Implementation repositories should point back to the protocol version and schema artifacts they target.\n\nKnown federation targets are listed in [federation-targets.json](federation-targets.json). These are discoverable online substrate sources that Workspace tools can clone, identify by branch and commit, and mount into another substrate without absorbing their records.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/registry/README.md",

    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-schemas-readme.md",

      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Schemas\r\n\r\nThis directory contains the machine-readable registry and validation artifacts for XanaNode.\r\n\r\nThe current layout is versioned and split between canonical type registries and JSON Schemas that validate substrate metadata and reports.\r\n\r\nCore files:\r\n\r\n- [xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json](xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json)\r\n- [xananode-node-types.schema.v0.3.0.json](xananode-node-types.schema.v0.3.0.json)\r\n- [xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json](xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json)\n- [xananode-relationship-types.schema.v0.5.0.json](xananode-relationship-types.schema.v0.5.0.json)\n- [substrate-manifest.schema.json](substrate-manifest.schema.json)\n- [substrate-node.schema.json](substrate-node.schema.json)\n- [substrate-relationships.schema.json](substrate-relationships.schema.json)\n- [property-registry.schema.json](property-registry.schema.json)\n- [xananode-property-registry.v0.1.0.json](xananode-property-registry.v0.1.0.json)\n- [substrate-layout.schema.json](substrate-layout.schema.json)\n- [xananode-substrate-layout.v0.1.0.json](xananode-substrate-layout.v0.1.0.json)\n- [namespace.schema.json](namespace.schema.json)\n- [merge-report.schema.json](merge-report.schema.json)\n- [compatibility-report.schema.json](compatibility-report.schema.json)\n- [substrate-diff.schema.json](substrate-diff.schema.json)\n- [author-profile.schema.json](author-profile.schema.json)\n- [nanopublication.schema.json](nanopublication.schema.json)\n- [ro-crate-metadata.schema.json](ro-crate-metadata.schema.json)\n\r\nThe versioned registry files define the canonical core node, relationship, property, and substrate layout conventions. The schema files describe the required structure of each registry plus related substrate and compatibility documents.\n\nSchemas and schema registries are licensed under `Apache-2.0` so implementations can adopt them freely while preserving attribution and provenance.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/schemas/README.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:dd085f93c87166a671d409a35823ebe52037ba0055fa6e63bc81e5ed2ba81481",

    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-compatibility-levels.md",
      "title": "Specs / Compatibility Levels",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "A protocol specification covering Compatibility Levels.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/compatibility-levels.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/compatibility-levels.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Compatibility Levels\n\nCompatibility levels describe how safely one implementation can ingest or federate another substrate.\n\n## Level 0: Opaque\n\nThe substrate can be stored as raw data, but its schema is unknown.\n\n## Level 1: Manifest Recognized\n\nThe substrate declares a manifest and namespace.\n\n## Level 2: Core Valid\n\nThe substrate validates against XanaNode Core.\n\n## Level 3: Extensions Declared\n\nThe substrate uses custom types, but those types are declared in namespaced extension schemas.\n\n## Level 4: Merge Candidate\n\nThe substrate can participate in identity matching, schema mapping, and merge reporting.\n\n## Level 5: Federation Ready\n\nThe substrate supports stable IDs, provenance, declared schemas, merge reports, and compatibility reports.\n\nCompatibility is not a moral ranking. It is a practical statement about how much automated interoperation is safe.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/compatibility-levels.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:9e8e8ee8beaf1a7d1f3fc872d4c60dc17a7c7713c737009b85da5a3bdd38cc29",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-27T16:35:32.999Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/compatibility-levels.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:9e8e8ee8beaf1a7d1f3fc872d4c60dc17a7c7713c737009b85da5a3bdd38cc29",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-federation.md",
      "title": "Specs / Federation",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "A protocol specification covering Federation.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/federation.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/federation.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Federation\n\nFederation is the process of connecting independently authored XanaNode substrates.\n\nFederation does not require one canonical truth source.\n\nInstead, it preserves local substrates and creates mappings among them.\n\n## Federation Inputs\n\n- substrate manifests\n- node registries\n- relationship registries\n- custom extension schemas\n- provenance metadata\n- merge reports\n\n## Federation Outputs\n\n- compatibility reports\n- identity mappings\n- schema mappings\n- conflict reports\n- combined analysis views\n\n## Core Rule\n\nNever merge by destroying context.\n\nMerge by adding structure.\n\n## Pack Composition\n\nFederation, local pack ingress, and substrate merging share one protocol model. A pack is a bounded substrate or substrate fragment that can be composed with another substrate in one of three modes:\n\n- `mounted`: keep the external governance boundary. The receiving substrate may render, traverse, cite, and analyze the pack without copying it into canonical local authorship.\n- `imported`: copy pack records into generated local artifacts while preserving provenance and pack metadata. Import does not imply duplicate collapse or identity reconciliation.\n- `merged`: reconcile the incoming pack with local records through explicit identity mappings, merge candidates, conflict reports, and review policy.\n\nMounting is reversible and is the default for optional packs. Importing is an auditable intake step. Merging is a stronger federation operation and must preserve disagreement, provenance, and local context instead of overwriting them.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/federation.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:a197a48cf7aba6a0a0cc1a75587edfedeca59676283ee3039710eaddc23bbd6f",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-27T16:35:32.999Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/federation.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:a197a48cf7aba6a0a0cc1a75587edfedeca59676283ee3039710eaddc23bbd6f",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-implementation-compatibility.md",
      "title": "Specs / Implementation Compatibility",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "A protocol specification covering Implementation Compatibility.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/implementation-compatibility.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/implementation-compatibility.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Implementation Compatibility\n\nAn implementation may describe itself as XanaNode-compatible when it validates, emits, imports, or projects XanaNode protocol artifacts according to the canonical specification and registries.\n\nImplementations should identify themselves clearly and reference the canonical specification:\n\n```text\nThis project is a XanaNode-compatible implementation.\nCanonical specification: https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode\n```\n\nIf the implementation supports only part of the protocol, it should say so:\n\n```text\nThis project is a partial XanaNode-compatible renderer for mounted substrate packs.\n```\n\nImplementations should not imply they are the canonical XanaNode project unless they are maintained as part of the canonical project. Compatibility means interoperability, not ownership.\n\nRecommended metadata:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"implements\": \"xananode-protocol\",\n  \"compatibility\": \"xananode-compatible\",\n  \"specification\": \"https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode\",\n  \"supported_artifacts\": [\"substrate.json\", \"nodes.json\", \"relationships.json\"]\n}\n```\n\nThis convention supports legal attribution, community attribution, brand recognition, and independent federation.\n\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/implementation-compatibility.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:ae0a610b3ab7207f0daa026baa104b1b95aaf85b96634b0fdf44a738a6b7de09",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-27T16:35:32.999Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/implementation-compatibility.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:ae0a610b3ab7207f0daa026baa104b1b95aaf85b96634b0fdf44a738a6b7de09",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-interoperability.md",
      "title": "Specs / Interoperability",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "A protocol specification covering Interoperability.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/interoperability.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/interoperability.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Interoperability Profiles\n\nXanaNode keeps its author-facing JSON simple, but it should map cleanly to established standards.\n\n## JSON-LD\n\nThe canonical JSON-LD context lives at [../contexts/xananode.context.jsonld](../contexts/xananode.context.jsonld).\n\nImplementations should use the context when exporting nodes, relationships, fragments, and substrate packages into Linked Data environments.\n\n## Web Annotation Selectors\n\nFragment selectors should use Web Annotation selector names where possible:\n\n- `TextQuoteSelector`\n- `TextPositionSelector`\n- `FragmentSelector`\n- `CssSelector`\n- `XPathSelector`\n- `RangeSelector`\n\nThe older `semantic-anchor` selector remains acceptable for human-authored stable anchors.\n\n## PROV-O\n\nXanaNode provenance fields map to PROV-O concepts:\n\n- nodes and relationship assertions are `prov:Entity`\n- people, organizations, projects, and external actors are `prov:Agent`\n- creation, assertion, review, import, and publication are `prov:Activity`\n- `created_by` maps to `prov:wasAttributedTo`\n- `created_at` maps to `prov:generatedAtTime`\n- `derived_from` maps to derivation provenance\n\n## Content IDs\n\nHuman-readable protocol IDs remain the stable author-facing identifiers. Implementations may add immutable content identifiers:\n\n- `protocol_id`: stable XanaNode ID\n- `content_id`: immutable hash or CID-like identity for the content\n- `version_id`: version identity such as a Git commit/path reference\n\nContent IDs should not replace protocol IDs. They support verification, fixity, and preservation.\n\n## Preservation Packages\n\nSubstrates may include RO-Crate-compatible companion metadata named `ro-crate-metadata.json`. This metadata should describe the substrate package, schema references, rights, authorship, fixity, and dependencies.\n\n## Nanopublication Profile\n\nHigh-value claim nodes may be exported in a nanopublication-like package separating:\n\n- assertion\n- provenance\n- publication information\n\nThis is optional but useful for scholarly, legal, scientific, and archival substrates.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/interoperability.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:ee9ccb3e98730c558dff0e2adc272bce9055430542e634e77524dfef8fd79a1e",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-27T16:35:32.999Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/interoperability.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:ee9ccb3e98730c558dff0e2adc272bce9055430542e634e77524dfef8fd79a1e",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-merge-validation.md",
      "title": "Specs / Merge Validation",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "A protocol specification covering Merge Validation.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/merge-validation.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/merge-validation.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Merge Validation\n\nMerge validation checks whether multiple substrates can be analyzed together.\n\nIt should verify:\n\n- each substrate has a namespace\n- each node has a stable ID\n- relationship types are declared\n- custom types are declared\n- required fields exist\n- target nodes can be resolved or marked external\n- conflicts are reported rather than silently overwritten\n\nA merge report is not a final truth statement. It is a record of what the merge process found.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/merge-validation.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:bc771268de15b40321daa2a8c7f5432a7882d31f2931b59403c48e14a8636676",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-27T16:35:32.999Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/merge-validation.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:bc771268de15b40321daa2a8c7f5432a7882d31f2931b59403c48e14a8636676",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-namespaces.md",
      "title": "Specs / Namespaces",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "A protocol specification covering Namespaces.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/namespaces.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/namespaces.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Namespaces\n\nNamespaces prevent independently authored substrates from colliding.\n\nA namespace identifies who owns a local vocabulary or substrate.\n\nExamples:\n\n```text\nxananode:claim\nexample.merge:report/federation-example-001\nexample.researcher_a:concept/knowledge-substrate\n```\n\nThe core namespace is `xananode`.\n\nCustom substrates should use their own namespace.\n\nA namespace should be declared in a substrate manifest and in any extension schema files.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/namespaces.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:ba8fec7ddf921b1f75b58104e07b8710f97a77bc53d40cdc1c2c99a030d18beb",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-27T16:35:32.999Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/namespaces.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:0d580444753d0982d4d36eedda0d56b4e2aa1a7d3cb5a30624fefd78a6d8bdd1",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-nodes.md",
      "title": "Specs / Nodes",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "A protocol specification covering Nodes.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/nodes.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/nodes.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Nodes\r\n\r\nNodes are addressable knowledge objects.\r\n\r\nA node may represent a person, concept, claim, question, hypothesis, problem, knowledge gap, communication, response, source, observation, essay, media item, event, place, organization, project, technology, publication, community, relationship, revision, trail, schema, or fragment.\n\r\nNodes should have stable IDs and human-readable summaries.\r\n\r\nNodes should carry provenance metadata when it is known. Common provenance fields include `created_by`, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `source_url`, `rights_status`, and `confidence`.\r\n\r\n`created_by` should point to a person, organization, project, or external actor identifier when possible. If the actor is local to the substrate, it should be represented as a node.\r\n\r\nA node is not automatically true. It is an object in the substrate that can be connected to evidence, claims, sources, disputes, and revisions.\n\n## Inquiry And Knowledge States\n\nKnowledge work is not only a collection of asserted facts. It also contains questions, provisional explanations, missing evidence, and unresolved problems. XanaNode models these as first-class nodes so a substrate can show what it knows, what it does not know, and what it is actively trying to find out.\n\nUse `question` for an explicit inquiry or prompt. A question may be answered, partially answered, raised by a source, asked in a communication, or connected to a knowledge gap.\n\nUse `hypothesis` for a provisional explanation or prediction. A hypothesis should be connected to tests, evidence, counterevidence, validation, falsification, and revision history.\n\nUse `problem` for a difficulty, risk, design challenge, or research problem that organizes work.\n\nUse `knowledge_gap` for a known absence: missing citation, unknown author, lost document, incomplete context, unresolved identity, or evidence that has not been found yet.\n\nCommon epistemic workflow properties include `uncertainty_level`, `review_status`, `research_priority`, and `evidence_strength`. These fields are also listed in the property registry so tools can parse them consistently.\n\nExample:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"id\": \"example.minimal:question/why-relationships-matter\",\n  \"title\": \"Why do relationships matter?\",\n  \"type\": \"question\",\n  \"status\": \"open\",\n  \"importance\": 5,\n  \"summary\": \"An inquiry into why preserving relationship structure changes what a knowledge system can do.\",\n  \"uncertainty_level\": \"medium\",\n  \"review_status\": \"needs_review\",\n  \"research_priority\": 4,\n  \"relationships\": []\n}\n```\n\n## Communication Nodes\n\nCommunication should not always be flattened into a simple edge such as `authored` or `discusses`. A communication can have topology, participants, directionality, feedback, medium, audience, and consequences. When those properties matter, model the communication itself as a `communication` node.\n\nUse `communication_model` to describe participant topology:\n\n- `one_to_one`\n- `one_to_many`\n- `many_to_one`\n- `many_to_many`\n\nUse `communication_pattern` to describe the semantic pattern:\n\n- `dialogue`\n- `broadcast`\n- `collection`\n- `coordination`\n- `negotiation`\n- `debate`\n- `consensus`\n- `instruction`\n- `query_response`\n- `publication`\n\nUse `response` for an answer, reply, rebuttal, clarification, or follow-up. Responses can `answers` a question, `replied_to` another response or communication, or be `answered_in` a communication context.\n\n## Primary Type And Facets\n\nEach node has one primary `type`. The primary type controls the node's stable route, required fields, default rendering, and validation behavior.\n\nNodes may also carry a narrower `subtype`. The subtype does not replace the primary type. It describes a more specific kind inside that route and validation category. For example, a node can remain a `source` while declaring `subtype: \"git_repository\"`, `subtype: \"official_site\"`, `subtype: \"documentation\"`, `subtype: \"support_page\"`, or `subtype: \"web_snapshot\"`.\n\nUse `subtypes` when one node needs multiple narrow labels. Subtypes should use lower-case slugs. Namespaced subtypes are allowed for extension vocabularies.\n\nSome real knowledge objects naturally play several roles. A quotation can be a `fragment` of a source, evidence used like a `source`, and an assertion that functions like a `claim`. Do not duplicate the quotation into separate nodes just to satisfy those categories. Model one canonical node and add secondary `facets` when the object should participate in more than one role.\n\nExample:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"id\": \"example.minimal:fragment/as-we-may-think-0004\",\n  \"title\": \"Associative Trails Quote\",\n  \"type\": \"fragment\",\n  \"facets\": [\"source\", \"claim\"],\n  \"summary\": \"A stable quotation fragment that can be cited as evidence and interpreted as an assertion.\"\n}\n```\n\nUse relationships to explain why a facet matters in context. For example, a quote can `supports` a claim, `derived_from` a source, or `defines` a concept. Facets describe secondary behavior; relationships describe meaning.\n\nProjection layers should show those secondary roles instead of hiding them. The primary `type` keeps authority over routing, validation, and default presentation, but a graph projection should visually mix the primary type color with any facet colors it recognizes. In other words, a quote that is primarily a `fragment` and also has `source` and `claim` facets should not look like only a fragment. Its visual mark should intertwingle the fragment, source, and claim colors by slices, bands, rings, or another accessible mixed-color treatment.\n\nIf a projection cannot use color, it should preserve the distinction with labels, texture, stroke patterns, badges, or another cue. A renderer may simplify the treatment at tiny sizes, but it should not erase the fact that the node carries multiple roles while claiming full projection compliance.\n\n## Media Assets\n\nMedia should be represented as `media` nodes, then connected to other nodes with `has_primary_media`, `depicts`, `represents`, `transcribes`, or another appropriate relationship. A node that wants a representative image should use `primary_media` to reference a media node rather than embedding a renderer-specific image path.\n\nPortable media nodes should use a relative `asset_path` when the file is part of the substrate or pack:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"id\": \"example.minimal:media/hugo-site-snapshot\",\n  \"title\": \"Hugo Site Snapshot\",\n  \"type\": \"media\",\n  \"subtype\": \"web_snapshot\",\n  \"media_type\": \"screenshot\",\n  \"mime_type\": \"image/png\",\n  \"asset_path\": \"assets/sources/hugo-official-site/snapshot.png\",\n  \"asset_role\": \"source_snapshot\",\n  \"source_url\": \"https://gohugo.io/\",\n  \"source_snapshot\": {\n    \"captured_at\": \"2026-06-19T00:00:00Z\",\n    \"source_url\": \"https://gohugo.io/\",\n    \"method\": \"screenshot\",\n    \"tool\": \"xananode-core\"\n  },\n  \"rights_status\": \"external\",\n  \"importance\": 3,\n  \"summary\": \"A captured visual representation of the Hugo official site.\",\n  \"relationships\": []\n}\n```\n\n`asset_path` is always relative to the substrate or pack root. It must not escape that root. Implementations that build packs must copy referenced assets into the pack and preserve the relative path, or rewrite the path and record that rewrite in pack metadata.\n\nMedia nodes should include stable content identifiers, usually `content_id: \"sha256:...\"`, whenever a tool can compute them. Content identifiers let federation tools detect duplicate files, distinguish duplicate bytes from duplicate titles, and explain how a file moved through different substrates.\n\nRecommended asset layout:\n\n```text\nassets/\n  media/\n  projection/\n    node-types/\n      <type>.svg\n    node-subtypes/\n      <type>/\n        <subtype>.svg\n  sources/\n    <source-node-local-id>/\n      snapshot.png\n      thumbnail.png\n      metadata.json\n```\n\nSource page previews, Open Graph images, screenshots, transcripts, and archived copies must preserve provenance, but they do not always require a second node. Use these rules:\n\n- if the captured file is itself the cited knowledge object, model it as one `source` node that may also carry the `media` facet, `media_type`, `mime_type`, `asset_path`, and related provenance fields\n- if the captured file is a distinct representation of another source object, use a separate `media` node and relate it back to the source with the appropriate relationship and provenance\n- if a renderer only needs the file as a projection aid and the file has no independent semantic role, keep it as an attached asset instead of elevating it to a new node\n\n## Type And Subtype Projection Media\n\nNode type registries may define projection media metadata under each node type's `projection` object. This lets projection layers draw a stable visual mark for a type without inventing renderer-local mappings.\n\nThe canonical fields are:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"type\": \"concept\",\n  \"projection\": {\n    \"icon\": \"concept\",\n    \"icon_label\": \"C\",\n    \"asset_path\": \"assets/projection/node-types/concept.svg\",\n    \"subtype_asset_path_template\": \"assets/projection/node-subtypes/concept/{subtype}.svg\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nWhen a substrate or pack includes the actual icon file, the icon file must be preserved as an asset. It only needs its own `media` node when the icon is being discussed, governed, versioned, cited, or otherwise treated as a knowledge object in its own right. Otherwise the schema/type node may simply reference the asset through its projection metadata.\n\nOfficial XanaNode projections use this order for a node's center mark:\n\n1. primary media when the node has an explicit `primary_media` or resolved visual media\n2. subtype projection media when a subtype-specific asset is available\n3. node type projection media\n4. registry `icon` or `icon_label` fallback\n\nThe node title remains the title and should be shown outside the center mark. Type and subtype labels may be shown as subtle chips, legends, tooltips, or inspector metadata. They should not replace the title.\n\n## Branding As Knowledge\n\nBranding is part of substrate identity and provenance. A logo, icon, color palette, tagline, or author mark should not live only inside renderer configuration. If a project, organization, author, or substrate changes its branding, that change has context and should be traceable.\n\nRepresent brand assets as `media` nodes, usually with `subtype: \"logo\"` or `subtype: \"image\"`. Reference those media nodes from substrate manifests or author profiles through their `branding` blocks:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"branding\": {\n    \"brand_name\": \"XanaNode\",\n    \"tagline\": \"Relationships preserve knowledge\",\n    \"icon_media\": \"example.minimal:media/xananode-icon\",\n    \"primary_color\": \"#55D6BE\",\n    \"accent_color\": \"#FF8C00\",\n    \"reason\": \"Protocol branding is modeled as substrate metadata and media.\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nRenderers should treat branding metadata as a projection input, not as the canonical source. The canonical source is the profile or manifest plus the referenced media node and its asset/provenance fields.\n\n## Software And Build Metadata\n\nSoftware projects, SDKs, renderers, workspace layers, desktop applications, and generated packs should carry version and build provenance when known.\n\nUse `software_version` for the package or release version. Use `build_metadata` for the exact build context:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"software_version\": \"0.1.0\",\n  \"build_metadata\": {\n    \"git_commit\": \"abc123\",\n    \"git_branch\": \"main\",\n    \"built_at\": \"2026-06-19T00:00:00Z\",\n    \"built_by\": \"@xananode/core\",\n    \"runtime\": \"node\",\n    \"dependencies\": [\n      {\n        \"name\": \"@xananode/core\",\n        \"version\": \"0.1.0\",\n        \"relationship\": \"uses\"\n      }\n    ]\n  }\n}\n```\n\nCompiled applications should record the version and compile/build date in their own metadata and, when represented in a substrate, on the project or technology node that describes that application. Generated canonical packs should include enough build metadata to identify which protocol, core, renderer, and workspace versions produced them.\n\n## Open Properties\n\nSubstrate nodes may carry extra root-level properties so authors can describe domain-specific facts without waiting for a protocol release. Common extra properties should use the canonical property registry when possible.\n\nThe core property registry standardizes fields such as `birth_date`, `geo_coordinates`, `currency_value`, `measurement_si`, and `external_identifier`. Implementations can use `schemas/xananode-property-registry.v0.1.0.json` to parse these values consistently while still accepting custom extension properties.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/nodes.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:258b7a00b5e128d3db5ab717a20581e9f8cefcdc4b09543ae136cf87d4e2e21e",

      "content": "# Specs\r\n\r\nThis directory contains the normative protocol definitions for XanaNode.\r\n\r\nThe spec set should be read alongside the versioned registries in [schemas/](../schemas), especially the core node and relationship registries.\r\n\r\nKey documents:\r\n\r\n- [xananode-core.md](xananode-core.md)\r\n- [substrates.md](substrates.md)\r\n- [nodes.md](nodes.md)\r\n- [relationships.md](relationships.md)\r\n- [namespaces.md](namespaces.md)\r\n- [federation.md](federation.md)\r\n- [merge-validation.md](merge-validation.md)\r\n- [transclusion.md](transclusion.md)\r\n- [tumbler-addressing.md](tumbler-addressing.md)\n- [compatibility-levels.md](compatibility-levels.md)\n- [interoperability.md](interoperability.md)\n- [studio.md](studio.md)\n- [implementation-compatibility.md](implementation-compatibility.md)\n\nThe current core registry files are versioned and define the protocol vocabulary used throughout the repository.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/README.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:a7d0d63d0cd4d091260eec44895c930d06278ba1dbcbaf0816770033ed2bea28",
      "source_snapshot": {

    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-relationships.md",
      "title": "Specs / Relationships",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "A protocol specification covering Relationships.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/relationships.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/relationships.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Relationships\r\n\r\nRelationships connect nodes with explicit meaning.\r\n\r\nXanaNode relationships are typed.\r\n\r\nExamples:\n\n- `supports`\n- `contradicts`\n- `explains`\n- `answers`\n- `investigates`\n- `requires_source`\n- `possibly_related_to`\n- `created`\n- `derived_from`\n- `communicated_to`\n- `transcludes`\n- `deep_links_to`\n\r\nRelationships may be simple edges or first-class nodes when the relationship itself needs evidence, provenance, dispute, or revision history.\r\n\r\nRelationship records should preserve assertion provenance when available. Common fields include `asserted_by`, `asserted_at`, `confidence`, `evidence`, and `tumbler`.\n\n`asserted_by` should point to a person, organization, project, or external actor identifier when possible. If the actor is local to the substrate, it should be represented as a node.\n\nRelationships may also carry temporal validity when the relationship was true during a specific historical interval. Use `valid_from` for the beginning of the interval and `valid_to` for the end. These fields describe the modeled reality, while `asserted_at` describes when the substrate recorded the assertion.\n\n## Projection Style\n\nRelationship type registries define canonical projection style metadata:\n\n- `color`\n- `inverse_color`\n- `line_style`\n- `inverse_line_style`\n- optional `projection` media metadata\n\nProjection layers use these values for connective lines, arrows, trails, legends, and other relationship marks. This is not decoration only: visual stratification helps readers distinguish evidence, lineage, authorship, governance, uncertainty, communication, and other relationship families before reading every label.\n\nA protocol-compliant projection may adapt contrast for accessibility, theme, or media constraints, but it must preserve the registry-level distinction between relationship types and their inverse readings. If a renderer cannot display color, it should use line style, labels, or another accessible cue instead of flattening every relationship into the same visual treatment.\n\nRelationship type registries may also declare projection media:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"type\": \"supports\",\n  \"category\": \"evidence\",\n  \"projection\": {\n    \"icon\": \"supports\",\n    \"icon_label\": \"->\",\n    \"asset_path\": \"assets/projection/relationship-types/supports.svg\",\n    \"category_asset_path\": \"assets/projection/relationship-categories/evidence.svg\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nRelationship projection assets should be carried as `media` nodes when bundled in a substrate or pack. Use `asset_role: \"relationship_type_projection_icon\"` for a specific relationship type and `asset_role: \"relationship_category_projection_icon\"` for category-level assets. Projection layers may use these assets in legends, path explainers, relationship catalogs, tooltips, and edge adornments, while edge color and line style still come from the relationship registry.\n\n## Inquiry, Uncertainty, And Workflow\n\nXanaNode relationships should preserve knowledge states, not only settled assertions.\n\nUse inquiry relationships when a node participates in research or explanation:\n\n- `answers`\n- `partially_answers`\n- `fails_to_answer`\n- `raises`\n- `investigates`\n- `motivates_inquiry`\n- `tests`\n- `falsifies`\n- `validates`\n\nUse workflow and uncertainty relationships when a node needs additional work:\n\n- `requires_information`\n- `requires_source`\n- `requires_review`\n- `requires_validation`\n- `requires_context`\n- `insufficient_evidence`\n- `unresolved`\n- `under_review`\n- `speculative`\n\nThese relationships make gaps explicit. A substrate should be able to say \"this claim needs a source\" or \"this question remains unresolved\" without pretending the missing knowledge is already known.\n\n## Discovery And Cognitive Relationships\n\nNot every useful connection is strong enough to be evidence, influence, or identity. Early research often begins with low-commitment associations.\n\nUse discovery and cognitive relationships for provisional connective tissue:\n\n- `possibly_related_to`\n- `shares_pattern_with`\n- `analogous_to`\n- `resonates_with`\n- `generalizes`\n- `specializes`\n- `abstraction_of`\n- `example_of`\n\nAuthors should prefer more specific high-commitment relationships when the evidence supports them. Low-commitment relationships are still valuable because they preserve the path by which a stronger interpretation may later be discovered.\n\n## Communication Relationships\n\nCommunication has structure. When the communication itself matters, represent it as a `communication` node and connect participants, questions, responses, audiences, and consequences with typed relationships.\n\nCanonical communication relationships include:\n\n- `asked`\n- `asked_in`\n- `answers`\n- `answered_in`\n- `replied_to`\n- `broadcast_to`\n- `communicated_to`\n- `presented_to`\n- `discussed_with`\n- `debated_with`\n- `negotiated_with`\n- `announced`\n- `reported_to`\n\nThis lets a substrate model knowledge flow: who asked, who answered, where the exchange happened, how it reached an audience, and what claims, projects, or gaps emerged from it.\n\n## Inverse Views\n\nCanonical relationship types are authored in one direction only. A type may name an `inverse` label for display or query purposes, but that inverse does not need to be registered as a second canonical type.\n\nFor example, an implementation can display `created_by` when viewing the target of a `created` relationship, but the stored relationship type remains `created`. This avoids bloating the canonical registry and avoids dangling inverse-type pairs.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/relationships.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:a7326e802d258a20e3567cdb6aa9e4de9b0b85fa86686c9f3ba8e075651bb4bc",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-27T16:35:32.999Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/relationships.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:a7326e802d258a20e3567cdb6aa9e4de9b0b85fa86686c9f3ba8e075651bb4bc",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }

      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-studio.md",

      "artifact_path": "specs/studio.md",

      "content": "# XanaNode Studio Guidance\n\nXanaNode Studio is an editor concept for local-first knowledge substrate authoring.\n\nIt should be understood as:\n\n> VS Code for knowledge substrates, hiding Git until the user is ready.\n\n## Principles\n\n- Do not reinvent Git.\n- Use Git as the substrate versioning layer.\n- Present Git concepts in human language first.\n- Keep the published substrate preview close to the real Hugo/static output.\n- Make validation actionable rather than cryptic.\n\n## Git UX Translation\n\n| Git concept | Studio language |\n|---|---|\n| commit | save snapshot |\n| branch | draft path |\n| merge | bring changes together |\n| pull request | propose changes |\n| diff | what changed |\n| log | history |\n\nPower users may reveal the Git layer directly.\n\n## Core Workspace\n\nStudio should use a three-panel model:\n\n- catalog and tools\n- live substrate view\n- node editor\n\nThe catalog is not a file tree. It should provide views by node type, trail, source, author, status, confidence, relationship cluster, recent edits, validation issues, unlinked nodes, and contradicted claims.\n\n## MVP\n\nThe first useful Studio should support:\n\n- create or open a substrate repo\n- show a node catalog\n- edit node content and metadata\n- add relationships with autocomplete\n- run validation\n- preview the Hugo implementation\n- save snapshots using Git\n- publish to GitHub Pages\n\n## Assistants\n\nFuture Studio assistants may include:\n\n- friendly validation repair\n- relationship suggestion\n- fragment maker\n- trail builder\n- source importer\n- media importer\n- review workflow\n- conflict UI\n- extension marketplace\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/studio.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:9e041388f1da9c6b3c40048f3850d7bb61482f4939b25681e2fadc7f05aa8df6",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-27T16:35:32.999Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/studio.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:9e041388f1da9c6b3c40048f3850d7bb61482f4939b25681e2fadc7f05aa8df6",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
        "tool": "@xananode/core"
      }
