    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-substrates.md",
      "title": "Specs / Substrates",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "A protocol specification covering Substrates.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/substrates.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/substrates.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Substrates\n\nA substrate is an independently authored XanaNode-compatible knowledge graph.\n\nA substrate includes:\n\n- a manifest\n- a declared namespace\n- a Git repository backing the substrate history\n- nodes\n- relationships\n- schema declarations\n- optional custom extensions\n- optional merge reports\n- optional mounted or imported substrate layers\n\n## Standard Layout\n\nA substrate root is the directory containing `substrate.json`. A hand-built substrate should be usable from that root without private tool state.\n\nThe machine-readable layout registry is:\n\n- `schemas/substrate-layout.schema.json`\n- `schemas/xananode-substrate-layout.v0.1.0.json`\n\nThe minimal required file is:\n\n```text\nsubstrate.json\n```\n\nThe standard production layout is:\n\n```text\nsubstrate.json\nauthor-profile.json\nrelationships.json\nnodes/\n  *.json\nnodes.json\ncontent/\n  nodes/\n    *.md\nassets/\n  media/\n  sources/\n  projection/\nschemas/\n  *.json\nreports/\n  *.json\npacks/\n  <mounted-substrate>/\nimports/\n  <incoming-material>/\nro-crate-metadata.json\n.xananode/\n  workspace.json\n  authors.json\n```\n\nProtocol JSON (`nodes/*.json`, `nodes.json`, and `relationships.json`) is the interchange surface. Markdown under `content/nodes/` is an authoring projection used by current tools. A substrate can be authored directly as protocol JSON and still be valid.\n\nTools should preserve recognized files even when they do not understand every extension. Unknown local files under `assets/`, `schemas/`, `reports/`, `packs/`, or `imports/` are part of the substrate unless sharing or export policy explicitly excludes them.\n\n## Git Backing\n\nEach production substrate is a Git repository. Git provides the substrate's version history, branching, review, merge, and synchronization layer.\n\nXanaNode does not replace Git. It defines knowledge-specific artifacts inside a repository:\n\n- `substrate.json`\n- `nodes-index.json`\n- `relationships.json`\n- `nodes/*.json`\n- optional `schemas/*.json`\n- optional merge and compatibility reports\n\nFor static-host discovery, a substrate should also expose a machine front door such as `/.well-known/xananode`, plus ordinary crawl hints like `robots.txt` and head links that point to the protocol artifacts. The human viewer may live at the root, but the machine handshake should make the substrate obvious without scraping page prose or client-side graph code.\n\nThe substrate manifest must include a `repository` block describing the Git remote and default branch. Example fixtures may live in subdirectories of the protocol repository, but independently maintained substrates should use the repository root as the substrate root.\n\nSubstrates are sovereign. They can be moderated independently while still participating in federation.\n\n## Portable Substrates And Composition\n\nIn current XanaNode language, the substrate is the primary unit. A `.substrate` file is a portable bundled substrate for transmission or release. This specification still uses `pack` in a few field names and compatibility notes because older tools shipped that wording first, but official user-facing tools should teach substrate, `.substrate`, mount, import, merge, and Intertwingle.\nA portable substrate is a set of XanaNode protocol artifacts that can be added to another substrate without losing the ownership boundary of the source substrate.\n\nPortable substrates use the same artifact shapes as a normal substrate:\n\n- optional `substrate.json`\n- `nodes/*.json` or any JSON file containing one node, an array of nodes, or `{ \"nodes\": [...] }`\n- `relationships.json` or any JSON file containing `{ \"relationships\": [...] }`\n- optional `assets/` files referenced by media node `asset_path` fields\n- optional schemas, reports, and companion metadata\n\nPortable substrates may be used in three composition modes:\n\n- `mounted`: the incoming substrate is included at build or analysis time, but remains externally governed. Its records are not copied into the receiving substrate as canonical local content.\n- `imported`: the incoming substrate's records are copied into generated local artifacts with provenance, but identity is not reconciled beyond explicit namespace mappings. Imported records must retain `imported_from`, substrate identity, and composition mode metadata.\n- `merged`: the incoming substrate is reconciled with the receiving substrate through identity mapping, duplicate detection, conflict handling, and review policy. Merged substrates should produce a merge or intake report.\n\nMounted substrates are the right model for optional example layers, lineage overlays, alternate interpretations, and domain extensions. Imported substrates are the right model when a substrate owner wants local generated artifacts for an external substrate without claiming authorship. Merged substrates are the right model when a substrate owner decides that incoming records should be reconciled with local canonical authorship.\n\n`absorbed` is a legacy alias for `imported`. New manifests should prefer `imported` or `merged` so tools can distinguish copying from identity reconciliation.\n\nThe substrate manifest may declare substrate references in `imports`:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"imports\": [\n    \"xananode:core\",\n    {\n      \"id\": \"example.minimal\",\n      \"source\": \"vendor/xananode-core/vendor/xananode-protocol/examples/minimal-substrate\",\n      \"mode\": \"mounted\",\n      \"version\": \"0.2.0\",\n      \"required\": true\n    }\n  ]\n}\n```\n\nString imports remain valid for simple vocabulary or schema dependencies. Object imports are used when the import is a concrete substrate source.\n\nThe field name is `imports` for manifest compatibility, but user interfaces may describe these records as mounted substrates, federation sources, or substrate mounts. The important distinction is governance: a mounted substrate remains someone else's authored source unless an explicit import or merge step changes that status.\n\nSubstrate ingress may declare namespace mappings:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"id\": \"xananode.lineage\",\n  \"source\": \"imports/lineage\",\n  \"mode\": \"mounted\",\n  \"namespace_mappings\": [\n    {\n      \"from\": \"xananode.example\",\n      \"to\": \"xananode.com\",\n      \"scope\": \"relationships\",\n      \"reason\": \"Bind example-authored relationship endpoints to the receiving canonical substrate namespace.\"\n    }\n  ]\n}\n```\n\nNamespace mappings are local federation rules. They do not rename the imported substrate's own nodes unless `scope` is `all`; with `scope: \"relationships\"` they only rebase relationship endpoints so mounted records can connect to equivalent local substrate nodes. Implementations should report mappings in review or merge/intake output so authors can audit how a mounted substrate is being interpreted.\n\nImplementations must not silently treat a mounted substrate as imported or merged. If a tool copies mounted records into local artifacts, it should record that as an explicit import step with review metadata. If a tool reconciles identity, de-duplicates nodes, or resolves conflicting claims, it should record that as a merge step. This prevents two repositories from accidentally claiming canonical ownership of the same governed records.\n\nWhen a substrate is cloned from an online federation target, the mounted name must include the Git branch and commit that were actually used, such as `Example Substrate (main@1a2b3c4d5e6f)`. Branch plus commit is the concrete version. A branch name alone is not enough provenance because the branch can move.\n\n## Substrate Media Portability\n\nPortable substrates that include local media must include the media files alongside the node records. Media files belong under the substrate root, normally in `assets/`. Media nodes reference those files with relative `asset_path` values.\n\nSubstrate builders must:\n\n- copy every file referenced by a media node `asset_path`\n- copy authored local substrate files that the substrate carries as evidence or media, including images, PDFs, audio, video, diagrams, source snapshots, and other digital files unless sharing policy excludes them\n- reject or warn on paths that escape the substrate root\n- preserve `source_url`, `rights_status`, `license`, `source_snapshot`, and content identifiers when known\n- keep `primary_media` references pointing at media node ids, not raw file paths\n- include projection media assets referenced by node type, subtype, relationship type, and relationship category registries when the substrate claims to carry those registries\n- report duplicate local files by content hash so authors can see when the same literal file has propagated through multiple substrates\n- report path conflicts when two different files claim the same relative `asset_path`\n\nSubstrate consumers must resolve `asset_path` relative to the source substrate root for mounted substrates and relative to the receiving substrate root for imported or merged assets after an explicit copy step.\n\n## Artifact Promotion Governance\n\nPortable substrates should preserve important local files, but tools must distinguish between preserving a file and promoting that file into its own node.\n\nA raw repository or workspace file should be promoted to its own node when it has independent semantic weight inside the substrate, for example when it is:\n\n- a protocol, schema, registry, compatibility, or governance artifact\n- a citable spec, proposal, policy, source document, or canonical example\n- a report, bundle, manifest, or machine-facing artifact that other tools may need to inspect directly\n- a file whose contents are meaningfully discussed, reviewed, disputed, transcluded, or traversed as part of the graph\n\nA raw file should usually remain an attached asset instead of becoming its own node when it mainly serves as:\n\n- implementation support\n- build or packaging glue\n- test scaffolding\n- projection inventory such as icons, thumbnails, sprites, static screenshots, or generated output\n- provenance attached to a higher-level source, media, schema, project, or substrate node\n\nLive source URLs and captured media are often related, but not always separate objects. A `source` node may point to `https://example.com` and also carry a local captured file when the captured file is the actual evidentiary object being cited in the substrate. Use a separate `media` node only when the capture is a distinct representation, derivative, preview, depiction, or auxiliary asset rather than the source object itself.\n\nWhen two mounted substrates carry the same literal file, tools should preserve provenance from both substrates instead of blindly copying both files forever. Permanent federation or merge may keep one canonical media node, record duplicate-file evidence in the merge report, and use transclusion or media relationships from the other substrate back to the retained file. That keeps the file lineage visible without multiplying identical bytes.\n\nLinking to an online file is allowed, but a portable substrate should prefer a local captured asset or a reference to a media node in another mounted substrate when the file matters to the knowledge claim. Online links rot; substrate-carried media preserves the evidence path.\n\n## Substrate Archives\n\nA portable substrate may be transmitted as a single `.substrate` archive. The archive is the whole substrate unit: manifest, nodes, relationships, media, registry extensions, source snapshots, and workspace metadata needed to keep the files together.\n\nThe recommended media type is `application/vnd.xananode.substrate+json+gzip`. The archive should contain a JSON envelope compressed with gzip. Tools may support other containers later, but official XanaNode tooling should read and write this format.\n\nMounted substrate records should preserve:\n\n- repository URL\n- branch\n- commit hash\n- archive file name when exported\n- namespace\n- mounted/imported/merged/absorbed mode\n\n## Executable Tool Contract\n\nThe protocol does not require Node.js, Python, or any other language runtime. Those are implementation choices, not protocol rules.\n\nOfficial XanaNode tooling should converge on two boring machine entry points that other applications can call regardless of stack:\n\n- `xananode-core`\n- `xananode-workspace`\n\nThese may be delivered as native executables, self-contained packaged binaries, platform launchers, or language-specific wrappers, but the command contract should remain stable across forms.\n\nThe intent is simple:\n\n- Core handles protocol reading, validation, analysis, fragment generation, bundle writing, and projection-ready graph derivation.\n- Workspace handles substrate folder lifecycle, authoring roots, imports, working copies, federation intake, archive packaging, and build orchestration.\n\nApplications such as Studio, Mobile, Hugo helpers, AI agents, browser services, and third-party integrations should be able to call those tools without embedding the implementation language directly.\n\n### Required executable behavior\n\nOfficial executable implementations should:\n\n- accept ordinary filesystem paths to substrate folders, `.substrate` archives, `substrate-bundle.json`, and `substrate-bundle.jsonl`\n- return structured machine-readable output through JSON on stdout, JSON files on disk, or both\n- return non-zero exit codes on validation, parse, or runtime failure\n- preserve protocol semantics regardless of host language\n- expose build metadata such as version, commit, build date, and implementation identity\n- keep command names and output envelopes stable enough that one tool can replace another implementation without rewriting the whole caller\n\n### Recommended Core commands\n\nThe stable Core surface should include commands equivalent to:\n\n- `xananode-core validate`\n- `xananode-core inspect`\n- `xananode-core build`\n- `xananode-core bundle`\n- `xananode-core analyze-intake`\n- `xananode-core projection`\n\nExpected responsibilities:\n\n- `validate`: schema and integrity validation without mutating source records\n- `inspect`: summarize a substrate or portable substrate source\n- `build`: emit split protocol artifacts and optional bundle variants\n- `bundle`: emit a portable `.substrate` archive plus optional JSON and JSONL companions\n- `analyze-intake`: evaluate an incoming substrate against a receiving substrate for merge candidates, conflicts, suggested links, suggested transclusions, duplicate files, and health signals\n- `projection`: derive renderer-neutral graph/projection data from protocol artifacts\n\n### Recommended Workspace commands\n\nThe stable Workspace surface should include commands equivalent to:\n\n- `xananode-workspace init`\n- `xananode-workspace open`\n- `xananode-workspace build`\n- `xananode-workspace export`\n- `xananode-workspace intertwingle`\n- `xananode-workspace deintertwingle`\n- `xananode-workspace health`\n- `xananode-workspace snapshot`\n\nExpected responsibilities:\n\n- `init`: create a compliant substrate folder with declared authorship and repository metadata\n- `open`: open an existing local substrate folder or working copy\n- `build`: orchestrate Core builds for the active workspace\n- `export`: write `.substrate`, `substrate-bundle.json`, `substrate-bundle.jsonl`, and split artifact outputs from the same source substrate\n- `intertwingle`: mount or clone another substrate into a local workspace without silently claiming ownership\n- `deintertwingle`: remove mounted material that has not been explicitly merged, while warning about broken chains or lost references\n- `health`: compute substrate health, intake reports, chain break warnings, summary/body duplication warnings, and other authoring signals\n- `snapshot`: create a versioned workspace snapshot through the substrate's backing VCS\n\n### JSON-first machine I/O\n\nFor interoperability, executable implementations should support JSON-first invocation patterns.\n\nExamples:\n\n- pass arguments by filesystem path and flags\n- optionally accept JSON request files or stdin envelopes\n- write JSON result envelopes to stdout when `--json` is requested\n\nThe exact envelope format may evolve, but official tools should preserve these principles:\n\n- callers should not scrape prose output\n- success and failure should be obvious without reading human narration\n- structured warnings should be distinguishable from fatal errors\n- file outputs should be declared in the response\n\n### Relationship to language-specific packages\n\nJavaScript, Python, Rust, Go, Java, Kotlin, or other language packages may still exist as implementation surfaces. That is fine.\n\nBut the protocol-facing contract should be the executable behavior and the artifact formats, not a requirement that every downstream caller embed Node.js APIs directly.\n\nIn other words: the substrate is the protocol object, and the executable contract is the boring transport layer that lets many tools participate without sharing one language runtime.\n\nThe current reference stack now supports Windows launcher packaging for `xananode-core` and `xananode-workspace` as self-contained executables with sibling runtime trees. That packaging shape is still an implementation detail, but it proves the protocol boundary can be used without a separate Node install on the calling machine.\n\n## Federation Targets\n\nThe protocol registry may list known online federation targets. A federation target is a Git-backed substrate source that tools can discover, clone, validate, and mount into a local workspace.\n\nThe registry is a discovery aid, not a central authority. A substrate can federate with targets outside the registry as long as their manifests, schemas, and sharing rules validate.\n\nWorkspace-level tools are responsible for cloning, naming, caching, mounting, and exporting mixed substrates. Studio may expose those actions, but the substrate management behavior belongs below the projection layer.\n\n## Sharing Policy\n\nNodes are shareable by default. This matches the federation model: an authored substrate is expected to be portable unless an author marks specific records as private, restricted, or needing review.\n\nA substrate may declare a top-level `sharing` policy:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"sharing\": {\n    \"default_shareable\": true,\n    \"excluded_nodes\": [\"personal:person/private-contact\"],\n    \"excluded_trails\": [\"personal:trail/private-family-history\"],\n    \"rules\": [\n      {\n        \"selector\": { \"tag\": \"medical\" },\n        \"shareable\": false,\n        \"scope\": \"private\",\n        \"reason\": \"Contains sensitive personal context.\"\n      }\n    ]\n  }\n}\n```\n\nIndividual nodes may override that default:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"id\": \"personal:claim/something-i-can-share\",\n  \"sharing\": {\n    \"shareable\": true,\n    \"scope\": \"public\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nWhen a trail is excluded, tools should treat the trail node and the trail's listed members as excluded unless an individual node explicitly opts back in. When a relationship touches a non-shareable node, pack exporters must omit that relationship or replace it with an explicit redaction record if the export profile supports redactions.\n\nCore, Workspace, Studio, and official projection layers must respect `sharing` during export, publication, federation, mounted-pack preparation, and generated review reports. A renderer may show private records inside a local authoring session, but it must not publish them into static public artifacts.\n\n## Projection Governance\n\nProjection layers are lenses over a substrate. They may choose layout, typography, motion, interaction, and density, but official XanaNode projections must preserve protocol semantics:\n\n- relationship direction must be visible when edges are shown\n- relationship colors and line styles should come from the relationship type registry, with accessible alternatives when color alone is insufficient\n- node titles should remain titles; official graph projections should use node type/subtype projection media or primary media as the node center mark\n- node type, subtype, and multi-type/facet signals should remain distinguishable through projection media, color mixing, subtle chips, legends, tooltips, or inspector metadata\n- hop-depth controls should actually filter by graph distance from the focused node\n- when a reader travels along visible connected nodes, the projection should animate the walked route rather than dragging a decorative line\n- when a reader jumps to an unconnected or hidden node, the projection should make that leap legible with staged reveal by hop depth\n\nCustom renderers can be compatible without copying the official look, but they should not flatten away direction, authorship, provenance, or sharing policy.\n",

      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-transclusion.md",

      "summary": "A protocol specification covering Transclusion.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/transclusion.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/transclusion.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Transclusion\r\n\r\nTransclusion is inclusion by reference rather than duplication.\r\n\r\nIn XanaNode, transclusion should preserve:\r\n\r\n- source identity\r\n- attribution\r\n- version lineage\r\n- fragment address\r\n- rights metadata\r\n\r\nThe relationship type `transcludes` describes transclusion at the graph level. A viewer may derive the inverse view `transcluded_by` when looking from the fragment back to the consuming node.\n\r\nThe minimal interoperable form is:\r\n\r\n- a source node, such as `source/as-we-may-think`\r\n- a fragment node with `source_node`, `source_version_id`, `source_content_id`, `fragment_id`, `content_id`, `version_id`, `tumbler`, and `selector`\n- a consuming node, such as an essay or trail\r\n- a `transcludes` relationship from the consuming node to the fragment node\r\n\r\nThe `transcludes` relationship should carry the same versioned fragment `tumbler` used by the fragment node. This makes the transclusion point to a specific source version and fragment version rather than to a floating latest passage.\n\nSelectors may point to more than paragraph-like text. A transcluded fragment can identify:\n\n- an exact text quote\n- a word or character range\n- a page or region selection in a document\n- an audio or video time span\n- an image region or other media-native chunk\n\nThe durable rule is the same in every case:\n\n1. the consuming node points to a fragment node\n2. the fragment node preserves the versioned tumbler\n3. the selector carries the chunking detail for that source version\n\nThis is how XanaNode gets closer to the Nelson-style goal of reusing a precise span rather than only whole files or whole paragraphs.\n\nWhen a person pastes quoted material into an official XanaNode tool, the best-case authoring flow is not \"paste now, maybe relink later.\" The tool should prefer to create or reuse a fragment, record the `transcludes` relationship immediately, and preserve the source directionality at the moment of reuse.\n\nImplementations may begin with practical fragment references before attempting full Project Xanadu-style transclusion.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/transclusion.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:b35e2c6a656a9f86ab4eb33096d1fce982a9ec24af53d9723da7d8db56dd523c",

        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:a589820115ef0554c82f4fd41f6d50d4635f8204c745aba95ffbec24631794cc",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",

      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-specs-tumbler-addressing.md",
      "title": "Specs / Tumbler Addressing",
      "type": "source",
      "subtype": "protocol_document",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "A protocol specification covering Tumbler Addressing.",
      "artifact_path": "specs/tumbler-addressing.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/tumbler-addressing.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# Tumbler Addressing\r\n\r\nTumbler addressing refers to persistent, location-independent addressing of knowledge objects and fragments.\r\n\r\nThe goal is to address a thing even if:\r\n\r\n- files move\r\n- folders change\r\n- URLs change\r\n- renderers change\r\n- the substrate is mirrored or federated\r\n\r\nXanaNode begins with stable node IDs and a minimal fragment addressing profile.\r\n\r\n## Address Profile\r\n\r\nNode addresses use:\r\n\r\n```text\r\n<namespace>:<node-kind>/<local-id>\r\n```\r\n\r\nVersioned node addresses add an immutable version identity:\n\n```text\n<namespace>:<node-kind>/<local-id>@<version-id>\n```\n\nFragment addresses append a fragment selector and must preserve both the source node version and the fragment version:\n\n```text\n<namespace>:<node-kind>/<local-id>@<source-version-id>#fragment/<fragment-id>@<fragment-version-id>\n```\n\r\nRules:\r\n\r\n- `namespace` is a registered namespace such as `xananode` or `example.minimal`.\r\n- `node-kind` is the node type family used in the substrate path, such as `source`, `claim`, `concept`, `essay`, or `fragment`.\r\n- `local-id` is stable within the namespace.\n- `source-version-id` identifies the exact source node version being addressed, such as a Git commit/path identity or immutable content ID.\n- `fragment-id` is stable within the addressed node.\n- `fragment-version-id` identifies the exact fragment extraction/version, usually a content hash or generated fragment revision ID.\n- Fragment nodes must store `source_node`, `source_version_id`, `source_content_id`, `fragment_id`, `content_id`, `version_id`, and `tumbler`.\n- A `tumbler` field must preserve the full versioned node or fragment address when a record depends on persistent addressing.\n\r\nExamples:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nexample.minimal:concept/knowledge-substrate\nexample.minimal:source/as-we-may-think@git:8e47e70:examples/minimal-substrate/nodes/source-as-we-may-think.json#fragment/0004@sha256:example-as-we-may-think-fragment-0004\n```\n\nUnversioned node addresses are stable identities. Versioned node and fragment tumblers are fixed references to an exact historical chunk. A transclusion should use the versioned fragment tumbler, not the floating node identity.\n\n## Selector Granularity\n\nThe tumbler gives the durable address. The selector explains how the chunk was found inside the source.\n\nAcross the protocol, selectors are allowed to be more granular than a paragraph block:\n\n- `TextQuoteSelector` for an exact quoted span with optional prefix/suffix context\n- `TextPositionSelector` for character offsets inside a source version\n- `RangeSelector` for generic start/end ranges\n- `FragmentSelector` for a stable local fragment id\n- `MediaFragmentSelector` or `TimecodeRangeSelector` for audio/video segments, image regions, or other media-native chunking\n\nFor time-based media, the current interoperable shape is:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"type\": \"TimecodeRangeSelector\",\n  \"unit\": \"ms\",\n  \"start_ms\": 12340,\n  \"end_ms\": 18750,\n  \"start_timecode\": \"00:00:12.340\",\n  \"end_timecode\": \"00:00:18.750\"\n}\n```\n\nFor text or structured documents, the selector may instead use:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"type\": \"RangeSelector\",\n  \"unit\": \"word\",\n  \"start\": 120,\n  \"end\": 135\n}\n```\n\nThat means XanaNode's equivalent of \"start at this exact word\" is not a different address family. It is the same fragment tumbler plus a selector precise enough to identify the span within that specific source version.\n\nThis profile is intentionally small. Future versions may add richer selectors, byte ranges, media regions, and additional revision systems without invalidating these base addresses.\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/tumbler-addressing.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:5b621f0f1c8861b7a852efb7d128070bc8b8569a6325e7a067a1b90854c6bd65",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-27T16:35:32.999Z",
        "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/specs/tumbler-addressing.md",
        "method": "archive",
        "content_id": "sha256:5b2d89d2734577c4d07e02c7a9b7fcaa173484c7351f89ff7159c07547222047",
        "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",

      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# XanaNode Core\r\n\r\nXanaNode is an open protocol for interoperable knowledge substrates.\r\n\r\nThe core specification defines the shared vocabulary, object model, and protocol-level assumptions used across the rest of the repository.\r\n\r\n## Scope\r\n\r\n- the canonical node registry in [schemas/xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json](../schemas/xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json)\r\n- the canonical relationship registry in [schemas/xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json](../schemas/xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json)\n- the versioned JSON Schemas that validate those registries\r\n\r\n## Core Model\r\n\r\nThe current node registry centers on person, concept, claim, source, essay, and observation.\r\n\r\nThe current relationship registry centers on definitions, evidence, claim structure, lineage, identity, creation, participation, location, revision, and related semantic links.\r\n\r\nThe core model is designed to preserve provenance and disagreement rather than flattening distinct knowledge states into one asserted fact.\r\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/specs/xananode-core.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:dde21bd72733f48e0092eaccad02e9debdf7cb0ebdcbed436cb2367f5e53e23b",

      }

    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/protocol-artifact-trademark.md",

      "type": "source",

      "summary": "The trademark policy describing how the XanaNode name and logo may be referenced.",
      "artifact_path": "TRADEMARK.md",
      "source_url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Protocol/blob/main/TRADEMARK.md",
      "media_type": "document",
      "mime_type": "text/markdown",
      "asset_role": "canonical_raw_source",
      "rights_status": "CC-BY-4.0",
      "relationships": [],
      "content": "# XanaNode Trademark Policy\n\nThe XanaNode name, XanaNode logo, and related brand assets identify the canonical XanaNode protocol and project.\n\nYou may use the name XanaNode to make accurate factual statements, such as:\n\n- \"implements the XanaNode protocol\"\n- \"XanaNode-compatible\"\n- \"built with XanaNode schemas\"\n- \"uses XanaNode Core\"\n\nYou may not use the XanaNode name or logo in a way that implies endorsement, official status, or control by the XanaNode project unless that relationship is true.\n\nImplementations should identify themselves as independently authored unless they are maintained by the XanaNode project. For example:\n\n> ExampleApp is an independent XanaNode-compatible implementation.\n\nModified logos, confusingly similar marks, and product names that imply ownership of XanaNode itself are not permitted without permission.\n\n",
      "asset_path": "assets/raw/protocol/TRADEMARK.md",
      "content_id": "sha256:920cc1e4e4719f3383ec0c50e4eafba89f848b730b4d297b3a22ab576edde81c",
      "source_snapshot": {
        "captured_at": "2026-06-27T16:35:32.999Z",

    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:source/xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft",
      "title": "XanaNode As A Kernel For Human Thought Draft",
      "type": "source",
      "importance": 4,
      "summary": "A raw authored comparison that frames XanaNode as an open foundational substrate layer for knowledge in the way Linux became an open foundational kernel for computing.",
      "created_by": "xananode.canonical:person/christian-siefen",
      "created_at": "2026-06-22T00:00:00.000Z",
      "rights_status": "canonical-public",
      "content_id": "sha256:xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft-source",
      "version_id": "sha256:xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft-source-v1",
      "content": "When Linus Torvalds created Linux, he did not build a flashy consumer application. He built an open foundational kernel, a plumbing layer that handles how a computer operates, manages memory, and talks to hardware. Because that plumbing was open, standard, and free, it became the invisible engine underneath Android phones, enterprise cloud servers, smart TVs, and supercomputers.\n\nXanaNode is trying to do the same thing for knowledge. It is not a notes app trying to trap attention inside one interface. It is a substrate protocol and stack layer meant to let knowledge move between tools without losing its structure.\n\nThe Core SDK plays the role of shared plumbing. It is renderer-independent and handles the baseline rules of structure, relationship typing, fragment tracking, validation, and export so every projection does not have to reinvent them.\n\nThe substrate and the projection are intentionally separated. In Linux, people interact through distributions such as Ubuntu, Fedora, or Red Hat while the kernel remains the underlying contract. In XanaNode, the same substrate can be viewed through Studio, Mobile, Hugo, or a future projection that does not exist yet. The tool is a lens over the data, not the data itself.\n\nThat separation matters because it prevents knowledge from being held hostage by one product. If a proprietary notes tool collapses, changes pricing, or drifts away from its users, the knowledge goes with it. XanaNode keeps the substrate local, protocol-shaped, and portable, so another compatible projection can pick it up and keep going.\n\nFederation is the ecosystem layer. Linux did not become important because one company owned all computation. It mattered because many independent actors could build on a common base. XanaNode aims for the same pattern with independently authored substrates that can mount, compare, and merge without surrendering provenance, lineage, disagreement, or ownership.\n\nThe point is not another app. The point is an open substrate ecosystem for durable human-to-machine knowledge transfer.",
      "relationships": []
    },

    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical:trail/federated-stack-proof",
      "title": "Federated Stack Proof",
      "type": "trail",
      "subtype": "evidence_path",
      "importance": 5,
      "summary": "A short trail showing that the XanaNode stack proves federation by publishing its own layers as interoperable substrates.",
      "content": "Follow the stack from the protocol substrate into Core, then outward into Workspace, Studio, Mobile, and Hugo.
